items.feature: Legacy contest enumeration
items.behavior: The contest catalog exposes every TSimContest member in its declared order.

items.feature: Legacy run mode enumeration
items.behavior: Session lifecycle behavior accounts for every legacy TRunMode value.

items.feature: Legacy contest definitions
items.behavior: The contest catalog preserves the identity, ordering, exchange fields, editability, and defaults of every legacy contest definition.

items.feature: Legacy persisted settings
items.behavior: Settings import and persistence account for every INI key read, written, migrated, or deleted by the legacy implementation.

items.feature: Legacy main-form objects
items.behavior: The product retains the information, editable fields, controls, indicators, and presentation roles exposed by every legacy main-form object.

items.feature: Legacy main-menu commands
items.behavior: Every legacy menu command remains available through an equivalent keyboard-first or pointer-accessible workflow.

items.feature: Legacy main-form event bindings and handlers
items.behavior: Every legacy main-form event binding and referenced handler is represented by an equivalent semantic engine command or transient UX interaction.

items.feature: Legacy shortcuts and keyboard branches
items.behavior: Every declared shortcut and direct keyboard branch remains available with equivalent focus-sensitive behavior.

items.feature: Legacy QSO record and error model
items.behavior: The QSO log retains every recorded value, verified value, error classification, correction flag, multiplier, point, and duplicate indicator used by legacy results.

items.feature: Legacy logging, scoring, rate, correction, and result paths
items.behavior: Every legacy log operation and score-table definition remains represented in deterministic logging, correction, score, rate, and result behavior.

items.feature: Legacy simulation state models and transitions
items.behavior: Every contest, station, and simulated-operator state and observed state transition is represented in the deterministic session state machine.

items.feature: Legacy contest, station, and operator routines
items.behavior: Every production routine participating in contest advancement, message exchange, station behavior, operator decisions, and station collection management is represented by acceptance coverage.

items.feature: Legacy portable keying and DSP processing
items.behavior: Every legacy Morse envelope, mixer, filter, modulation, AGC, sound-data type, constant, property, and processing routine is represented by deterministic parity coverage.

items.feature: Legacy sound output, buffering, and WAV adapters
items.behavior: Legacy device selection, bounded buffering, playback lifecycle, WAV read/write, and adapter failure behavior remain available behind portable engine-owned audio contracts.

items.feature: Legacy VCL-only hint and volume controls
items.behavior: The permanent hint and volume slider interaction roles remain represented in the cross-platform UX without retaining VCL dependencies.

items.feature: Legacy contest-specific implementations
items.behavior: Every contest-specific type, property, and production routine remains represented in deterministic rule, exchange, multiplier, point, and validation behavior.

items.feature: Legacy call, prefix, exchange, and serial data parsers
items.behavior: Call-list lookup, DXCC lookup, exchange fields, serial generation, callsign utilities, lexical analysis, Sweepstakes exchange parsing, contest call-history loading, and every explicit parser path preserve their legacy data model and failure behavior.

items.feature: Legacy QSB and random effects
items.behavior: QSB envelopes and seeded random distributions remain represented by deterministic simulation and DSP acceptance vectors.

items.feature: Legacy score dialog
items.behavior: Score presentation and close behavior remain available in the cross-platform UX.

items.feature: Legacy data files, recording, export, and failure paths
items.behavior: Every bundled application or data asset, distribution-file contract, command-line and external integration, generated data file, recording and export path, and explicit file, persistence, dialog, and exception operation remains available with equivalent success and failure behavior.
legacySources: bundled data and application resource files

items.feature: Legacy Lazarus and Delphi form definitions
items.behavior: Every legacy form object, event binding, keyboard shortcut, resource reference, and published form member remains represented by an equivalent keyboard-first cross-platform workflow and observable UI outcome.

items.feature: Legacy project, package, and deployment metadata
items.behavior: Every Delphi and Lazarus application, package, test-project, deployment, resource, configuration, unit-membership, and lifecycle declaration needed to build and distribute the legacy product has an explicit cross-platform counterpart or verified replacement.

items.feature: Legacy unit and smoke test contracts
items.behavior: Every active, disabled, and commented legacy test declaration, registration, fixture, lifecycle hook, method, case, and Lazarus smoke check is reviewed and represented by executable cross-implementation coverage or an equivalent port acceptance case.

items.feature: Legacy Pascal unit lifecycle hooks
items.behavior: Every legacy initialization and finalization hook executes at an equivalent lifecycle boundary with deterministic ordering, state effects, registration behavior, cleanup, and failure handling.

behavioralObligations.behavior: Every CE contest identifier is present in declaration order and selects the same contest family.
Every contest exposes CE-equivalent names, exchange types, defaults, and feature flags.
Every CE run mode is present and retains its observable selection semantics.
Certified parity always executes the pinned CE oracle and never silently substitutes a committed fixture.
The dedicated XPlat parity target executes every applicable acceptance case exactly once.
CI checks out the pinned clean CE revision and builds the hash-verified oracle before certification.
Every case retains complete replay inputs, red evidence, and a reachable first-green commit.
Manifest status, inputs, outputs, evidence classification, hashes, and Git provenance are internally consistent.
The inventory covers CE forms, projects, resources, generated files, tests, commands, data, and failure paths.
The CE oracle selects each ActiveContest before observing contest-dependent metadata or behavior.
UX, settings, data, adapters, and runtime features are certified by executed behavior rather than source tokens or filenames.
Local sidetone is mixed at the CE pipeline stage and level before filtering, modulation, and AGC.
Non-Farnsworth Morse symbols, cut numbers, unit timing, five millisecond ramps, and mark-space transitions match CE exactly.
Every CE moving-average receiver stage, reset, cascade order, and filter-swap numeric vector matches exactly.
Carrier frequency quantization, phase advance, quadrature modulation signs, and modulator numeric vectors match CE.
AGC attack, release, gain equations, state transitions, reset behavior, and output numeric vectors match CE.
Complex remote-station BFO mixing uses CE-equivalent signs, channel order, phase application, accumulation order, and output normalization.
The default CE compatibility output contract is 11025 Hz mono, 512 samples per block, and identical sample totals.
The physical audio queue preserves CE-equivalent default depth, FIFO block order, startup fill order, and exact sample continuity.
MT19937 output and every CE random distribution primitive produce deterministic numeric vectors for reviewed seeds.
QSK applies CE-equivalent receiver ducking and recovery around local transmissions.
RIT changes each station's audible offset using CE range, steps, and block-boundary timing.
Runtime bandwidth changes replace or update receiver filters at the CE-equivalent block boundary.
QRM uses CE-equivalent randomized interfering CW stations, messages, levels, pitches, speeds, retries, and lifetimes.
QRN produces CE sparse impulses and burst stations before receiver filtering and AGC.
Every remote station has an independent CE-distributed QSB process without fading the receiver noise floor.
Flutter is the CE probabilistic fast per-station QSB mode rather than a global multiplier.
Remote station amplitude and pitch use CE distributions and deterministic draw ordering.
Station BFO phase advances and resets at the same CE transmission boundaries.
The production keyer primitive used by SST supports independent character and spacing speeds with CE-equivalent marker encoding, sample counts, default ramps, and block padding.
Selecting SST carries the configured sending and character speeds through settings, session state, station message sequencing, and the production renderer so operator audio uses CE Farnsworth timing.
One seeded session random stream reproduces CE ownership and cross-feature draw order.
CE block sizes from 128 through 2048 are supported or proven import-equivalent with matching timing.
Startup warmup requests, block numbering, prefill, and alternating-filter swap timing match CE.
Base complex hiss and receiver noise floor match CE level, spectrum, random draws, and processing order.
WAV headers, PCM scaling, rounding, clipping, negative full scale, and sample bytes match CE vectors.
Recording failure and queue backpressure stop recording without faulting or allocating in the contest render path.
Raw capture, WAV, null, and physical playback receive identical ordered pre-conversion audio blocks.
Physical callbacks, underruns, drops, device loss, recovery, stale callbacks, shutdown, and disposal are bounded and proven natively.
All-effects rendering retains allocation, latency, queue-depth, underrun, drop, and GC evidence representative of sustained play.
Operator transmission completion and remote interpretation occur only after the rendered CW envelope completes.
Caller creation follows completed CQ, TU, and MyCall events with CE Poisson and no-stop rules.
Start is silent and an empty Enter initiates the appropriate CE contest CQ.
CQ, TU, operator call insertion, and periodic station-ID messages match each CE contest.
Remote exchanges retain and format every CE contest identity field, cut number, repeat, and correction variant.
CWT, SST, Field Day, NAQP, Sweepstakes, WPX, HST, and all other contests include every required CE exchange field.
ALLJA and ACAG station truth use the correct CE data columns without serial fallback.
Sweepstakes models serial, weighted precedence, own call, check, section, copied values, truth, and errors.
ARRL DX and NAQP apply CE home-call filtering and dynamic location or exchange derivation.
WPX and HST station serials use CE elapsed-time, skill, and random behavior.
HST and WPX start constraints, forced settings, activity, bandwidth, duration, and valid serial rules match CE.
Single-call delay, caller lifetime, patience, retry, Gaussian WPM, calls-from-keyer, station-ID rate, and HST call-pool removal match CE.
Mid-message append, unsent callsign correction, and abort completion preserve CE timing and station notification.
Confidence, LID, Sweepstakes correction, call-and-number repeat, and F12 probability branches match CE.
Reset and restart clear and recreate the same authoritative state at the same ordered boundaries as CE.
All 12 contest constructors expose the same exchange enum pair, default own exchange acceptance, and Farnsworth flag as CE.
Logging without a completed matching live station produces CE-compatible NIL truth rather than fabricated truth.
A call enters worked-call state only when CE would accept verified truth, so an initial error does not poison correction.
Duplicate classification requires the same prior verified-correct QSO conditions as CE.
QSO records preserve true WPM, copied and true fields, per-field errors, corrections, and contest column flags.
Wrong, incomplete, NIL, B4, and corrected training QSOs are committed with CE message and logging order.
Raw and verified points, multipliers, score, rate, and correction totals match CE after every QSO.
Automatic completion, WPX history, HST append, historical score browsing, and submission workflows match CE.
Cabrillo export includes complete interoperable QSO fields for every supported contest.
Operators can validate, persist, reload, and use a distinct own exchange for every CE contest.
F2 and ESM transmit the configured operator exchange rather than received-entry values.
Entry labels, visibility, widths, limits, filters, and focus order adapt to each contest as CE does.
Contest-specific input transforms preserve valid text and apply equivalently to keys, paste, and IME input.
Wipe clears local fields and authoritative engine ESM or active-QSO state at one semantic boundary.
Escape or Abort resets active send and ESM state and notifies stations with CE completion semantics.
Punctuation and modified Enter preserve CE TU, log-only, wrong-QSO, and field-clearing workflows.
F9 and Run Pile-Up explicitly start Pile-Up regardless of the selected run-mode control.
Every live QSK, effect, activity, speed, pitch, bandwidth, and monitor change reaches the engine through the client.
RIT, WPM, and bandwidth ranges, increments, clamping, and displayed engine state match CE.
Visible shortcut labels and commands consistently distinguish F11 Wipe from Ctrl+F11 Score.
Physical plus, equals, keypad Add, and international layouts invoke only the CE-equivalent completion actions.
All CE key-down, key-up, shortcut, mouse-wheel, tuning-reset, and monitor-reset workflows are available.
Log rows expose copied and true values, corrections, NIL, B4, duplicate, error type, and chronological status accessibly.
Start, stop, and reset clear fields, restore focus, and transition pointer affordances exactly as CE.
Tab and Shift+Tab remain in the CE high-frequency entry workflow for every contest and state.
Numeric child controls, validation, status, and failure feedback expose names, values, actions, focus, and live announcements.
The native GUI remains usable at minimum size, 100, 150, and 200 percent scaling, high contrast, and platform fonts.
Score dialogs, automatic completion, history navigation, actions, focus, and accessible content match CE.
Every legacy hint, volume, and UI component behavior remains functionally available through the cross-platform UX.
Production startup invokes CE settings migration when appropriate.
Migration translates CE indexes, ordinals, zero-or-one booleans, IDs, Hz values, duration, block size, and contest exchanges.
Migration and save preserve unknown or unconsumed CE values and are idempotent.
Clean-profile call, WPM, activity, duration, pitch, bandwidth, and related defaults match CE.
Every CE duration from 1 through 240 minutes is accepted and persists without hidden clamping.
Users can replace CE reference data through an explicit root with packaged fallback and equivalent discovery.
Every parser rejects malformed rows and reports missing or invalid replacement data without fabricating values.
Call, prefix, exchange, serial, section, lexer, and contest data parsers produce CE-equivalent values and failures.
A CE-compatible fixed WAV naming, overwrite, and discovery workflow remains available.
About, engine, assembly, CLI, archive, icon, package, and evidence version provenance agree.
Release evidence launches and discovers a real native Avalonia main window rather than setup-only execution.
Release publication requires physical-audio startup, sustained playback, effects, device change, recovery, and shutdown evidence.
In-process and remote clients cannot mutate authoritative completed QSO or result state.
The shared application service enforces the documented single active interactive session contract.
Commands apply at every documented block boundary, including during long simulation advances.
Mutating request IDs replay safely and command idempotency retention is bounded.
Lossless events and coalescible snapshots use independent bounded delivery so slow observers cannot delay or silently lose authority.
Snapshots carry a sequence watermark and reconnect never delivers older events after newer state.
Fault, operator-message, score, rate, recording, result, and control-lease events expose every authoritative transition.
Lease acquire, renew, release, takeover, identity, ordering, and transport-neutral control behavior are complete.
Identical full-session vectors produce identical commands, events, snapshots, results, and failures through in-process and gRPC clients.
Native GUI, focus, routed-key, pointer, scaling, accessibility, and shutdown evidence exists on every supported platform.
TUI, CLI, in-process, hosted gRPC, WAV, data override, migration, and archive behavior are captured on clean systems.
Every supported platform must be complete before release promotion or artifact publication.
Final certification executes every obligation live with zero skipped, waived, quarantined, expected-failure, or fixture-only cases.
Structured CE and XPlat A/B sessions cover contests, audio conditions, corrections, logging, history, and migration with experienced users.
Enter with an empty, partial, corrected, or complete call selects and sends the same ESM message and state transition as CE.
Selecting a historical QSO updates the callsign-information region to the selected call as CE does.
The UX sends semantic duration and never owns sample-rate or simulation-block conversion policy.
Help, About, readme, website, and community actions match CE availability, targets, errors, keyboard access, and focus return.
Score browsing, submission, disabled or unavailable service behavior, failures, focus, and history match CE.
Session lifecycle transitions reject invalid starts such as rmStop, define close-while-running behavior, and emit the same ordered terminal state through every client.
Every supported CE key is consumed with equivalent semantics or preserved losslessly across import, save, and restart.
CLI help, version, validation failures, runtime failures, and successful commands expose stable documented output and exit codes in packaged builds.
Legacy initialization and finalization hooks execute with equivalent order, registration, cleanup, state, and failures.

cases.behavior: For the fixed seed 12345 vector, raw CE Random conversion to binary32, bounded integer Random including bound zero, and the CE RndUniform, RndUShaped, RndNormal, RndGaussLim, RndRayleigh, and RndPoisson routines produce the same first eight exact values, complete 4096-value distribution sequence hash, and post-group random-stream sentinel as the XPlat LegacyRandom and LegacyRandomEffects primitives.
preconditions: The pinned CE oracle and toolchain fingerprint are available.

The vector uses seed 12345, eight directly reported probes and 4096 total results per distribution group, integer bounds 0, 1, 2, 3, 10, 1000, 65536, and 2147483647, Gaussian mean 5.1 and limit 1.3, Rayleigh mean 2.7, and Poisson mean 3.3.
The CE adapter resets RandSeed to 12345 immediately before each raw or distribution group so every primitive starts from the same MT19937 state.
The raw group assigns each Random result directly to Single, and every floating-point result is compared by exact little-endian IEEE-754 binary32 bits.

The bounded-integer group executes the pinned FPC System.Random(LongInt) overload used by CE; this toolchain-owned overload has no RndFunc.pas inventory selector, while all CE distribution routines remain bound to their exact RndFunc selectors.
After each complete 4096-value group, the adapter records the next raw Random-to-Single value as a sentinel for the primitive's exact random draw consumption.
The XPlat adapter creates a fresh LegacyRandom for each group and invokes the production LegacyRandom and LegacyRandomEffects primitives without substituting recorded decisions.

assertions.firstDivergence: The zero-based first ordinal mismatch between fixture and observed string arrays, with null expected or actual only when one array ends first.

cases.behavior: For two fresh station-free CE receiver runs at the fixed compatibility vector with QSB disabled, enabling flutter changes neither of the first two normalized full audio blocks because CE uses flutter only while selecting a remote station's QSB bandwidth. The XPlat production engine must preserve the same exact receiver hiss and noise floor when flutter is enabled with no remote stations.
preconditions: The pinned CE oracle and toolchain fingerprint are available.

Each fresh CE runtime uses CQ WPX, rmStop, seed 12345, 11025 Hz, 512 samples per full block, 500 Hz bandwidth, 600 Hz pitch, no remote stations, no operator transmission, and QSB, QRM, QRN, and QSK disabled.
After contest construction, each CE run executes the real MainForm.SetBw path with handleless controls and resets RandSeed to 12345 immediately before its first GetAudio request.
Each CE run executes and verifies five real one-Single zero startup requests through TContest.GetAudio without resetting RandSeed before the two complete receiver blocks.

The two fresh CE runs differ only in Ini.Flutter. The station-free runtime constructs no TDxStation, and with Ini.Qsb false TContest.GetAudio reaches neither TDxStation.GetBlock nor TQsb.ApplyTo, so both normalized binary32 block sequences and their aggregate must remain bit-for-bit identical.

The XPlat adapter creates two fresh production engine sessions that differ only in SessionSettings.Flutter, keeps SessionSettings.Qsb false, starts and immediately aborts each session, verifies no audio before explicit advancement and zero active stations, then captures two complete blocks through IAudioSink.

This narrow case proves only that flutter does not multiply station-free receiver hiss. Positive per-station ownership, QSB gating, the 30 percent fast-mode selection, bandwidth distribution, draw order, independent envelopes, and runtime toggling remain uncovered, so audio.flutter-fast-per-station-qsb remains partial.

assertions.firstDivergence: The zero-based first mismatch is ordinal 3, flutter-block[0]. XPlat's session-global post-receiver flutter multiplier changes the station-free receiver block while CE leaves it identical to clean audio because CE flutter only selects a remote station's QSB bandwidth.

cases.behavior: At the fixed seed-24680 compatibility vector, an active K1ABC QRM station collides with ten scripted K1ABC caller candidates. CE constructs each complete TDxStation before collision testing, discards attempts 1 through 9, and accepts attempt 10 without a collision check. Every discarded candidate consumes its station, operator, speed, QSB, level, and pitch random sequence. The accepted caller retains the tenth catalog exchange and the shared random stream ends at ordinal 25373.
preconditions: The pinned CE v18 oracle and reviewed FPC 3.2.2 toolchain are available.
The fresh CE runtime uses CQ WPX, rmStop, seed 24680, 11025 Hz, 512 samples per block, station call W7SST, QRM enabled, and QRN, QSB, flutter, QSK, and LIDs disabled.
The scripted contest returns K1ABC for the active QRM station and for caller candidates 1 through 10. It assigns caller exchange rows EX01 through EX10 and records every completed GetExchange call.

TStations.AddCaller constructs a complete TDxStation before CallsignExists. Attempts 1 through 9 collide with the active QRM call and are freed. The retry counter reaches zero immediately after constructing attempt 10, so that final duplicate is accepted without calling CallsignExists.

Each TDxStation construction owns its R1, TDxOperator, receive speed, exchange, TQsb initialization and bandwidth refill, amplitude, and Gaussian pitch draws. Discarded candidates must therefore advance the authoritative shared stream exactly as live CE candidates do.

The internal XPlat probe requires manual timing, exact session revision and block, QRM enabled, and a fresh one-shot session. It runs on the session worker, creates one production QrmStation, invokes production AddCaller with a scripted catalog selector, and is absent from IMorseRunnerClient, snapshots, gRPC, and UX contracts.

This case certifies active-QRM collision visibility, complete-candidate retry construction, nine discards, unconditional tenth-attempt acceptance, exact retained exchange/operator fields, and the terminal random checkpoint. It does not certify general collision rates, non-QRM caller collisions, other contests, runtime audio mixing, or caller lifecycle after acceptance.

assertions.firstDivergence: The zero-based first mismatch is ordinal 2, catalog. The pre-implementation XPlat engine checks collisions only against normal callers, accepts the first K1ABC identity beside the active K1ABC QRM source, and constructs only that accepted caller after selection. CE selects eleven catalog rows total, fully constructs ten caller candidates, discards nine, and unconditionally accepts the tenth.

cases.behavior: At the fixed seed-1843 compatibility vector, the first live CE receiver block satisfies the QRM trigger and eagerly creates exactly one internal TQrmStation. The constructor consumes the shared random stream through message selection, selects LU5MT from the content-bound MASTER.DTA catalog, sends QRL?   QRL? at 31 WPM with the pinned amplitude and pitch, contributes the first 512 samples of its 53248-sample envelope to the same receiver block, and remains stSending. The XPlat production engine must reproduce the source-order random ownership, internal station metadata, message, same-block receiver output, and terminal checkpoint without exposing QRM as a public caller.
preconditions: The pinned CE oracle, toolchain fingerprint, and content-bound MASTER.DTA catalog are available.

Each fresh CE runtime uses CQ WPX, rmStop, seed 1843, 11025 Hz, 512 samples per complete block, 500 Hz bandwidth, 600 Hz pitch, station call W7SST, no normal DX stations, no operator transmission, and QRN, QSB, flutter, QSK, and LIDs disabled.

After contest construction, each CE run executes the real MainForm.SetBw path with handleless controls, resets RandSeed to 1843 immediately before audio framing, verifies five real one-Single zero startup requests, and captures exactly one complete receiver block.

The fresh CE runs differ only in Ini.Qrm. The complete block consumes 1024 complex-hiss draws before the QRM trigger at ordinal 1024. Seed 1843 yields binary32 bits 38e1bf40, which satisfies the 0.0002 creation threshold.

The live constructor consumes R1 ordinal 1025, patience ordinal 1026, catalog ordinal 1027, amplitude ordinal 1028, Gaussian pitch ordinals 1029 and 1030, WPM ordinal 1031, and message ordinal 1032. The next shared-stream checkpoint is ordinal 1033.
The content-bound MASTER.DTA is 1239476 bytes with SHA-256 acf37090e7c9c0f2146a2b08608295cb243c8bfe649a421d1c528a59656097aa and 46039 calls. Catalog index 23903 selects LU5MT.

The live TQrmStation has patience 2, R1 bits 3f03301e, amplitude 19583.306640625 with bits 4698fe9d, pitch offset -124 Hz, WpmS and WpmC 31, and message choice 2 selecting [msgQrl2] with text QRL?   QRL?.

The message encoder eagerly creates a 53248-sample, 104-block envelope. Station.GetBlock contributes its first 512 samples in the trigger block, advances SendPos to 512, leaves 103 complete blocks, and retains the station in stSending.

The clean normalized block hash is 3ba44162f2959aeeaa6599059e97033d942258e3a2e0dc33cbb07defbb12a4c5. The QRM block hash is 72f7618e7e055db7fefd472c47f0488046087905d5810b1e1aa97b88187f643d, with first final-output divergence at sample 310.

The XPlat adapter creates independent clean and QRM-enabled production MorseRunnerEngine sessions with automatic timing disabled, starts and immediately aborts each rmStop session, advances exactly one block, captures actual IAudioSink output, validates public caller snapshots and events remain empty, obtains one guarded internal QRM observation, and then reads the authoritative terminal random checkpoint.

The QRM observation seam is internal and parity-only. It requires manual timing plus exact session revision and simulation block, runs on the session worker, consumes no random value, advances no simulation state, and is absent from IMorseRunnerClient, public snapshots, gRPC, and UX surfaces. Before QRM production exists it returns an explicit empty observation.

This case certifies one first-block successful trigger, eager constructor draw order, one catalog selection, one level, pitch, speed, message, envelope length, same-block audibility, and terminal checkpoint. It does not certify trigger-rate statistics, other message branches or distributions, retries, timeout Gaussian behavior, complete lifetime, overlaps, normal-station interaction, RIT, or runtime toggling, so audio.qrm-interfering-cw-stations remains partial.

assertions.firstDivergence: The zero-based first mismatch is ordinal 3, station. XPlat replays the content-bound CE constructor decisions and captures the exact clean receiver path, but production does not yet create an internal QRM station when ordinal 1024 satisfies the trigger, so the guarded parity observation reports zero instead of one TQrmStation. The QRM block consequently remains equal to clean rather than first diverging at final-output sample 310, and the shared stream does not advance through constructor ordinal 1032.

cases.behavior: For two fresh station-free CE receiver runs at the fixed compatibility vector with QRN disabled, seed 12345 does not satisfy the first complete block's QRM construction trigger, so enabling QRM creates no TQrmStation and changes neither the normalized full audio block nor its raw-Single hash. The XPlat production engine must preserve the same exact receiver output when QRM is enabled but no interfering station is triggered.
preconditions: The pinned CE oracle and toolchain fingerprint are available.

Each fresh CE runtime uses CQ WPX, rmStop, seed 12345, 11025 Hz, 512 samples per full block, 500 Hz bandwidth, 600 Hz pitch, no normal DX stations, no operator transmission, and QSB, flutter, QRN, QSK, and LIDs disabled.
After contest construction, each CE run executes the real MainForm.SetBw path with handleless controls and resets RandSeed to 12345 immediately before its first GetAudio request.
Each CE run executes and verifies five real one-Single zero startup requests through TContest.GetAudio without resetting RandSeed before exactly one complete receiver block.

The two fresh CE runs differ only in Ini.Qrm. With Ini.Qrn false, the QRM construction trigger consumes shared random ordinal 1024 after the block's 1024 hiss draws. Seed 12345 yields approximately 0.17384081427007914, so the real production station collection must remain empty in both runs and the two normalized binary32 blocks must be bit-for-bit identical.
No second block is compared because the QRM-enabled run consumed the otherwise absent trigger draw and therefore begins its next block at a different shared-stream position.

The XPlat adapter creates two fresh production MorseRunnerEngine and EngineSession sessions that differ only in SessionSettings.Qrm, starts and immediately aborts each session, verifies no audio before one explicit simulation-block advance and zero active stations, then captures the complete block through the actual IAudioSink.

This narrow case proves only no-trigger output invariance. Positive QRM construction, construction probability and draw ownership, a shared-random sentinel, messages, levels, pitch, WPM, same-block audibility, retries, and lifetime remain uncovered, so audio.qrm-interfering-cw-stations remains partial.

assertions.firstDivergence: The zero-based first mismatch is ordinal 2, qrm-block[0]. XPlat's unconditional post-receiver QRM sine changes the station-free receiver block while CE adds no TQrmStation and leaves the first complete block identical to clean audio because seed 12345 does not satisfy the QRM trigger.

cases.behavior: For two fresh station-free CE receiver runs at the fixed compatibility vector, enabling QRN consumes one trigger draw per raw complex-noise sample, replaces the real component at the six source-order replay-derived indexes 92, 248, 323, 482, 488, and 507, consumes one value draw for each replacement, and consumes a final burst trigger that creates no TQrnStation. The live QRN receiver output differs from clean output first at normalized sample 310 after CE receiver filtering, modulation, and AGC. The XPlat production engine must reproduce the exact clean and QRN blocks, station counts, and terminal shared-stream sentinels.
preconditions: The pinned CE oracle and toolchain fingerprint are available.

Each fresh CE runtime uses CQ WPX, rmStop, seed 12345, 11025 Hz, 512 samples per complete block, 500 Hz bandwidth, 600 Hz pitch, no normal DX stations, no operator transmission, and QSB, flutter, QRM, QSK, and LIDs disabled. The paired runs differ only in Ini.Qrn.
After contest construction, each CE run executes the real MainForm.SetBw path with handleless controls and resets RandSeed to 12345 immediately before its first GetAudio request.

Each CE run executes and verifies five real one-Single zero startup requests through TContest.GetAudio without resetting RandSeed, then captures exactly one complete receiver block through the same live method.

A content-bound replay of Contest.pas source order consumes the 1024 complex-hiss draws, records QRN trigger ordinals 1116, 1273, 1349, 1509, 1516, and 1536, and records replacement-value ordinals 1117, 1274, 1350, 1510, 1517, and 1537. The resulting raw replacement indexes and values are replay-derived, while the live TContest.GetAudio hashes and terminal sentinels corroborate the complete observable CE path without instrumenting pre-filter state.

The live CE clean block hash is 6b468ab13ccc1accb6ec587b8a51d27ca23eb80b20bce034106e547ad3565378. The live CE QRN block hash is 16375b39a2a153bc44f33449bad640084f8dbed67c6b8bb9ccb3dec094be5435, and its first final-output divergence from clean is sample 310 after the two receiver filters, modulation, AGC, normalization, and clamping.

The QRN burst trigger is replay-derived ordinal 1542 with value 0.486590252, so the live block must retain zero stations. Immediately after each live GetAudio call, CE records the next shared-stream value: clean ordinal 1024 has Single bits 3e320354, and QRN ordinal 1543 has Single bits 3f43412e.

The XPlat adapter creates two fresh production MorseRunnerEngine and EngineSession instances with automatic timing disabled, starts and immediately aborts each rmStop session, verifies no audio before one explicit simulation-block advance and zero active stations, captures each complete block through the actual IAudioSink, then obtains one guarded terminal checkpoint from the authoritative session random source.

This narrow no-burst case proves first-block background impulse probability, replacement semantics, source-order draw ownership, final receiver output, no-burst station count, and terminal sentinels only. Triggered TQrnStation construction, duration, envelope impulses, amplitude, same-block audibility, ticking, destruction, later blocks, and runtime toggling remain uncovered, so audio.qrn-impulses-and-burst-stations remains partial.

assertions.firstDivergence: The zero-based first mismatch is ordinal 3, qrn-block[0]. XPlat draws QRN-enabled receiver hiss and its continuous post-receiver noise from an effect-specific stream instead of applying CE's six sparse real-component replacements on the authoritative shared stream before receiver processing. Later rows also expose the wrong terminal shared-stream position.

cases.behavior: At the fixed seed-1903 compatibility vector, the first live CE receiver block creates exactly one internal TQrnStation after the background QRN trigger. Its eager constructor consumes the next duration and amplitude draws, builds a two-block 1024-sample sparse envelope, contributes the first half of that envelope to the same receiver block, remains stSending after block one, contributes the second half to block two, and destroys itself during the second block tick. The XPlat production engine must reproduce the source-order random ownership, internal interference lifecycle, exact receiver output, and terminal shared-stream checkpoints without exposing QRN as a public caller or changing LastCaller and caller events.
preconditions: The pinned CE oracle and toolchain fingerprint are available.

Each fresh CE runtime uses CQ WPX, rmStop, seed 1903, 11025 Hz, 512 samples per complete block, 500 Hz bandwidth, 600 Hz pitch, QRN enabled, no normal DX stations, no operator transmission, and QSB, flutter, QRM, QSK, and LIDs disabled.
After contest construction, each CE run executes the real MainForm.SetBw path with handleless controls and resets RandSeed to 1903 immediately before its first GetAudio request.

Each CE run executes and verifies five real one-Single zero startup requests through TContest.GetAudio without resetting RandSeed. A fresh one-block run captures the ordinal-2576 terminal checkpoint, and an independent fresh two-block run captures both complete receiver blocks and the ordinal-4117 checkpoint.

A content-bound source-order replay consumes 1024 complex-hiss draws before each block's background QRN loop. Block one replaces the real component at indexes 154, 210, 245, 284, 324, 341, 424, and 493 and then consumes the true burst trigger at ordinal 1544.

The live TQrnStation object is inspected immediately after block one. Its eager constructor consumes duration ordinal 1545 with binary32 bits 3dc788c6, produces two blocks and 1024 envelope samples, consumes amplitude ordinal 1546, and stores amplitude binary32 bits 48e976cf.

The complete live TQrnStation envelope is cross-checked against the content-bound replay. Its only nonzero indexes are 359, 411, 848, 907, and 990, with binary32 bits 47c46069, 4849d6e3, c7e5b614, 476e2c1c, and c75e86f2. All other envelope samples remain positive zero.

After live block one, Stations contains exactly one object whose class is TQrnStation, state is stSending, and envelope length is 1024. Station.GetBlock has consumed its first 512 samples without truncating the envelope. After live block two, Station.GetBlock clears the completed envelope and Station.Tick invokes TQrnStation.ProcessEvent(evMsgSent), leaving zero stations.

The live normalized receiver block hashes are 41096894caafa0890ea1f5d18545aa14b644ed1e9f20aff31f9f8fcec75d960e and 44ae49f68a99e7688200685231b9cbf6c84414ae8a6b1b4736b78cce1d7d4637. Their aggregate binary32 hash is bec466358e35bf3720c074c9fee0ea8e7ef8f656164b6dd48d5758da66c41f61.

The fresh one-block terminal shared-stream checkpoint is ordinal 2576 with binary32 bits 3f58ce2d. The continuous two-block terminal checkpoint is ordinal 4117 with binary32 bits 3e9fed0d. The replay does not consume the one-block checkpoint while advancing the two-block sequence.

The XPlat adapter creates independent one-block and two-block production MorseRunnerEngine sessions with automatic timing disabled, starts and immediately aborts each rmStop session, advances one block at a time, captures audio through IAudioSink, verifies public ActiveStations stays empty at both boundaries, and obtains guarded terminal checkpoints from the authoritative session random source.

QRN burst lifecycle is not a public ActiveStationSnapshot contract. The adapter obtains count, sending state, and envelope length through the internal parity-only EngineSession observation seam guarded by session ID, expected revision, and expected simulation block. Before burst production exists, that seam returns an explicit empty observation while public caller snapshots remain empty.

The existing sparse-QRN vector remains the independent proof for no-burst background impulses. This triggered case adds one eager two-block burst construction and destruction vector only. Other seeds, duration boundaries, overlapping bursts, interactions with normal or QRM stations, and runtime QRN toggling remain uncovered, so audio.qrn-impulses-and-burst-stations remains partial.

assertions.firstDivergence: The zero-based first mismatch is ordinal 3, station-lifecycle. XPlat reproduces the block-one background QRN decisions but does not create internal burst interference when the ordinal-1544 trigger succeeds, so the guarded internal parity observation reports zero instead of one live two-block TQrnStation. Public ActiveStations correctly remains empty. XPlat consequently omits the constructor draws and burst envelope from receiver audio, advances the shared stream incorrectly for block two, and reaches different terminal checkpoints.

cases.behavior: For two fresh station-free CE receiver runs at the fixed compatibility vector, enabling QSB changes neither of the first two normalized full audio blocks because CE applies QSB only inside each remote station's GetBlock path. The XPlat production engine must preserve the same exact receiver hiss and noise floor when QSB is enabled with no remote stations.
preconditions: The pinned CE oracle and toolchain fingerprint are available.

Each fresh CE runtime uses CQ WPX, rmStop, seed 12345, 11025 Hz, 512 samples per full block, 500 Hz bandwidth, 600 Hz pitch, no remote stations, no operator transmission, and QRM, QRN, flutter, and QSK disabled.
After contest construction, each CE run executes the real MainForm.SetBw path with handleless controls and resets RandSeed to 12345 immediately before its first GetAudio request.
Each CE run executes and verifies five real one-Single zero startup requests through TContest.GetAudio without resetting RandSeed before the two complete receiver blocks.

The two fresh CE runs differ only in Ini.Qsb. With zero remote stations, TContest.GetAudio never reaches TDxStation.GetBlock or TQsb.ApplyTo, so both normalized binary32 block sequences and their aggregate must remain bit-for-bit identical.

The XPlat adapter creates two fresh production engine sessions that differ only in SessionSettings.Qsb, starts and immediately aborts each session, verifies no audio before explicit advancement and zero active stations, then captures two complete blocks through IAudioSink.

This narrow case proves only that QSB does not fade station-free receiver hiss. Per-station QSB construction, independent envelopes, distribution parameters, draw ownership, runtime toggling, and flutter remain uncovered, so audio.qsb-independent-per-station remains partial.

assertions.firstDivergence: The zero-based first mismatch is ordinal 3, qsb-block[0]. XPlat's session-global post-receiver QsbProcessor changes the station-free receiver block while CE leaves it identical to clean audio because CE QSB is owned by remote stations.

cases.behavior: A CE station owns its QSB process for its full lifetime, but TDxStation.GetBlock reads Ini.Qsb on every rendered block. Starting with QSB disabled and enabling it after two blocks therefore leaves the first two raw station-envelope blocks unchanged, applies the existing station's independent QSB state to blocks three and four, and advances the shared random stream only for the enabled blocks.
preconditions: The pinned CE oracle and toolchain fingerprint are available.

Two fresh CE runs use CQ WPX, rmStop, seed 12345, 11025 Hz, 512 samples per block, one scripted K1ABC TDxStation, 30 WPM, unit amplitude, zero pitch offset, and identical long Morse text. QRM, QRN, flutter, QSK, and LIDs are disabled.
TDxStation construction creates and warms its private TQsb instance even while Ini.Qsb is false, preserving CE constructor draw ownership.

The disabled run leaves Ini.Qsb false for four station-envelope blocks. The runtime-toggle run also leaves it false for blocks zero and one, then sets it true before TDxStation.GetBlock for blocks two and three without reconstructing the station.
Both runs capture the raw binary32 station envelope before receiver BFO mixing, filtering, modulation, AGC, and sink conversion, plus a terminal shared-random checkpoint.

The current XPlat station captures qsbEnabled at construction and exposes no semantic runtime QSB command, so the unchanged active station cannot begin applying its existing QSB processor at the CE block boundary.

input.messageText: K1ABC K1ABC K1ABC K1ABC K1ABC K1ABC K1ABC K1ABC

assertions.firstDivergence: The zero-based first mismatch is ordinal 1, disabled-block[0]. XPlat has no semantic runtime QSB control for an active session, and SimulatedStation freezes its qsbEnabled flag at construction, so the adapter reports the missing control boundary instead of synthesizing CE station-envelope output.

cases.behavior: For the fixed CE compatibility vector, the pinned CE TContest.GetAudio pure receiver path after normal SetBw filter configuration and the XPlat production engine emit the same normalized binary32 receiver-noise samples after filtering, modulation, and AGC across 12 consecutive 512-sample blocks.
preconditions: The pinned CE oracle and toolchain fingerprint are available.
The CE runtime uses CQ WPX, rmStop, seed 12345, 11025 Hz, 512 samples per block, 500 Hz bandwidth, 600 Hz pitch, no stations, no operator transmission, and every optional receiver effect disabled.

After contest construction, the CE adapter executes the real MainForm.SetBw path with handleless controls, matching normal CE startup and configuring both moving-average filters for 500 Hz before audio capture.
The first five one-Single CE startup requests are verified and discarded only to align the first complete receiver block; this case does not certify startup framing.
The CE random seed is reset immediately before capture, then 12 consecutive complete receiver blocks are observed through TContest.GetAudio.

The XPlat runtime starts and immediately aborts a production engine session, verifies that no audio is emitted before explicit advancement, then captures exactly 12 consecutive blocks through IAudioSink.

assertions.firstDivergence: The zero-based first ordinal mismatch between fixture and observed string arrays, with null expected or actual only when one array ends first.

cases.behavior: For the fixed station-free CE receiver vector, the first complete TContest.GetAudio block consumes exactly 1024 consecutive values from the one seeded process random stream, two values for every complex hiss sample. The immediately following random value is ordinal 1024 with CE binary32 bits 3e320354. The XPlat production engine must emit the identical receiver block through IAudioSink and expose the same final checkpoint from its authoritative session random stream.
preconditions: The pinned CE oracle and toolchain fingerprint are available.

The CE runtime uses CQ WPX, rmStop, seed 12345, 11025 Hz, 512 samples per complete block, 500 Hz bandwidth, 600 Hz pitch, no normal DX stations, no operator transmission, and QSB, flutter, QRM, QRN, QSK, and LIDs disabled.
After contest construction, the CE adapter executes the real MainForm.SetBw path with handleless controls and resets RandSeed to 12345 immediately before its first GetAudio request.

The CE adapter executes and verifies five real one-Single zero startup requests through TContest.GetAudio without resetting RandSeed, then captures exactly one complete receiver block through the real TContest.GetAudio path.

CE generates each of the block's 512 raw complex hiss samples with two consecutive Random calls before filtering, modulation, AGC, and normalization. The adapter then performs exactly one final Random call, verifies its underlying source value is 0x2c80d4e4, and records the rounded Single bits 3e320354 at zero-based ordinal 1024.

The XPlat adapter creates a production MorseRunnerEngine and EngineSession with automatic timing disabled, starts and immediately aborts the rmStop session, verifies no audio before one explicit simulation-block advance and zero active stations, and captures the complete block through the actual IAudioSink.

After validating the final public SessionSnapshot, the XPlat adapter makes one final internal parity-only checkpoint request on the session loop, guarded by the observed revision and simulation block. The checkpoint draws exactly one value from the authoritative session random source and does not alter public Domain, client, or transport contracts.

This case proves receiver-hiss ownership and the first complete block's draw order only. Cross-feature ordering for QRN, QRM, station construction, QSB, flutter, calls, exchanges, and later blocks remains explicitly pending, so audio.single-seeded-random-stream remains partial.

assertions.firstDivergence: The zero-based first mismatch is ordinal 2, shared-random-checkpoint. XPlat emits the exact CE receiver block because its effect-specific random source begins from the same seed, but its authoritative session random source remains untouched and returns binary32 bits 3f6dfb52 instead of CE bits 3e320354 after the block's 1024 hiss draws.

cases.behavior: For the CE compatibility profile, the pinned CE SST station path and the XPlat production keyer primitive at 15 WPM with 25 WPM character timing produce the same true and block-padded sample counts and raw Single envelope bytes for two fixed messages.
preconditions: The pinned CE oracle and toolchain fingerprint are available.
The schema-v2 fixture was captured from the pinned clean CE reference.
The CE side executes the real SST station send path; the XPlat side invokes the shipped production keyer primitive directly.
Each side uses a fresh keyer for every fixed message at 11025 Hz, 512 samples per block, and output amplitude 300000.

assertions.firstDivergence: The zero-based first ordinal mismatch between fixture and observed string arrays, with null expected or actual only when one array ends first.

cases.behavior: For one fresh initial run at the fixed CE compatibility vector, physical continuous playback performs four synchronous one-Single prefill requests and one completion-driven one-Single refill before the first full block, then the receiver produces 16 consecutive normalized binary32 blocks with filter swaps after absolute blocks 10 and 20. This case observes startup framing and receiver phase, not WAV contents, runtime bandwidth changes, or stop/restart continuity.
preconditions: The pinned CE oracle and toolchain fingerprint are available.

The fresh CE runtime uses CQ WPX, rmStop, seed 12345, 11025 Hz, 512 samples per full block, 500 Hz bandwidth, 600 Hz pitch, AlSoundOut1.BufCount 4, no stations, no operator transmission, and QRN, QRM, QSB, flutter, and QSK disabled.

After contest construction, the CE adapter executes the real MainForm.SetBw path with handleless controls and sets RandSeed to 12345 immediately before absolute request 1; it does not reset the seed between the five startup requests and the 16 full requests.

The first four GetAudio calls reproduce the synchronous TAlSoundOut.Start prefill callbacks. Absolute request 5 reproduces the first TAlSoundOut.BufferDone completion-driven refill, and absolute request 6 is the first full block.

The device-free CE adapter derives the four synchronous callbacks from the inherited TAlSoundOut buffer array, virtualizing only Start's hardware-open boundary. It routes later callbacks through the inherited TAlSoundOut.BufferDone refill branch. Its OnBufAvailable recorder owns all 21 real TContest.GetAudio calls and records callback origin, request number, samples, and filter roles.

The CE adapter verifies five one-Single zero startup frames and then records full blocks for absolute requests 6 through 21. SwapFilters runs after absolute blocks 10 and 20. It does not execute Enabled, DoSetEnabled, TWaitThread, WinMM, PutData, or WAV writes.

The five startup frames are CE physical presentation framing before canonical full blocks. This case does not exercise WAV, raw, or null sink implementations; their exclusion remains separately pending.

The XPlat adapter reads PhysicalAudioSink.GetDiagnostics startup framing without initializing a device, derives every startup row and aggregate prefix from that production metadata, captures the first 16 production IAudioSink blocks beginning at simulation block 0, and maps only those full blocks to CE absolute requests 6 through 21 for a phase-aligned DSP comparison. The current diagnostics contract truthfully reports no startup frames.

This vector excludes runtime bandwidth changes and stop/restart behavior. CE restart repeats the five startup requests after resetting BlockNumber, preserves filter histories and roles, modulator phase, AGC memory, and RitPhase, and clears remote stations through Log.Clear; that continuity remains a separate pending case.

assertions.firstDivergence: The current XPlat observation first differs at the prefill summary because no five one-Single physical startup requests are represented. After canonical startup framing aligns the 16 full-block rows, full indexes 0 through 14 match and full index 15 at absolute block 21 first differs at sample 310 because XPlat counts filter phase from full block 1 instead of CE absolute block 6.

cases.behavior: For all 12 legacy contest IDs in declared order, the selected contest implementation exposes the same sent and received exchange-field enum pair, accepts the same catalog default own exchange, and enables Farnsworth timing for the same contests.
preconditions: The pinned CE oracle and toolchain fingerprint are available.
The schema-v2 fixture was captured from the pinned clean CE reference.
The 12 contest IDs are evaluated in their legacy declaration order.

assertions.firstDivergence: The zero-based first ordinal mismatch between fixture and observed string arrays, with null expected or actual only when one array ends first.

cases.behavior: For fixed empty, short-partial, uncertain, corrected, repeated, and complete call actions, the live CE Enter path and the real headless XPlat MainWindow path select the same semantic messages and expose the same focus, question-mark selection, entry-field, call-retention, and QSO-count state. The XPlat observation stops at messages synchronously accepted into the engine queue and does not claim rendered or emitted audio.
preconditions: The pinned CE oracle and toolchain fingerprint are available.
The CE side invokes the handleless MainForm.FormKeyDown VK_RETURN path and drains the queued station transmission through GetBlock.

The XPlat side invokes the real headless Avalonia MainWindow Enter route, observes actual TextBox focus and selection, and validates EnterSendMessageResult queued semantic messages without advancing simulation blocks.
Every reset action creates a fresh CQ WPX pileup runtime; corrected and same-call-repeat actions continue the preceding runtime.
No remote caller station is created, so the observation isolates operator Enter and ESM behavior.

assertions.firstDivergence: The zero-based first ordinal mismatch between fixture and observed string arrays, with null expected or actual only when one array ends first.
